source_packet.title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
source_packet.audience: Readers who understand dot-product attention and want the paper's formulation, implementation, and empirical findings.
source_packet.central_question: Can absolute position be encoded in query and key representations while their inner product depends explicitly on relative position?

section_policy.sequence_rule: Begin with why self-attention needs position, let the historical equation thread produce the functional requirement, derive and implement the rotary solution, then separate algebraic properties, empirical evidence, and limitations.

paper_excerpts.text: The paper narrates a specific equation lineage: the Transformer adds learned or sinusoidal absolute position before projection (Eqs. 3-4); Shaw et al. add a learned relative key term (Eq. 5); Transformer-XL decomposes content and relative-position interactions (Eqs. 6-7); T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa retain, remove, or disentangle terms (Eqs. 8-10). The authors' shared diagnosis is that prior work modifies an additive decomposition, motivating a multiplicative construction.
Equation 11 formulates the relative-position requirement. Equations 14-16 give the rotary solution and show that the query-key inner product contains R at relative position n minus m.

Table 1 reports 27.5 BLEU for RoFormer and 27.3 for Transformer-base on WMT 2014 English-to-German. Figure 3 reports faster convergence in the shown language-model pre-training and Performer experiments.

code_notes.text: The official pseudocode constructs the perpendicular companion, applies q cos plus q-perpendicular sin and the analogous key operation, then takes the standard attention dot product.

agent_input.claim_policy: Use the paper's terms: formulation of relative position encoding, rotary position embedding, sequence length flexibility, long-term decay, and linear self-attention. Attribute every related-work equation to its predecessor paper and distinguish what changed, what remained additive, and which question led to Eq. 11. Keep exact algebra separate from empirical findings and extrapolation claims.

concept_graph.thesis: RoFormer asks whether absolute position can be encoded in query and key representations while their inner product depends explicitly on relative position; its answer is rotary position embedding, a multiplicative rotation of query and key feature pairs whose dot product contains the relative position n−m.

nodes.label: self-attention
nodes.summary: Dot-product self-attention needs a way to distinguish token order. The paper frames position encoding as the mechanism that lets attention scores depend on where tokens occur, not only on token content.

nodes.label: learned or sinusoidal absolute position
nodes.summary: Eqs. 3–4 describe the Transformer construction in which learned or sinusoidal absolute position is added before projection. The position signal is additive in the token representation.

nodes.label: Shaw et al.
nodes.summary: Eq. 5 adds a learned relative key term to the attention score. The change is to insert relative position into the score, while the mechanism remains an additive modification of attention components.

nodes.label: Transformer-XL
nodes.summary: Eqs. 6–7 decompose attention into content and relative-position interactions. The paper uses this as part of the lineage of additive or decomposed score terms.

nodes.label: T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa
nodes.summary: Eqs. 8–10 are presented as variants that retain, remove, or disentangle terms in the expanded attention score. In the RoFormer paper's diagnosis, they still work within an additive decomposition rather than replacing it with a multiplicative construction.

nodes.label: formulation of relative position encoding
nodes.summary: Eq. 11 states the functional requirement: encode absolute positions into query and key representations so that their inner product is a function of the relative position between indices.

nodes.label: two-dimensional rotary solution
nodes.summary: Eqs. 12–13 solve the Eq. 11 requirement in the complex plane. Position becomes a rotation, making the relative-position behavior geometric rather than an added score term.

nodes.label: rotary position embedding
nodes.summary: Eqs. 14–16 extend the rotary construction to d-dimensional query and key vectors by pairing adjacent coordinates. Separate absolute rotations R_m and R_n enter the query and key, and orthogonality collapses the inner product to a matrix at relative position n−m.

nodes.label: RoPE attention pseudocode
nodes.summary: The official README constructs cos_pos and sin_pos, creates the perpendicular companion by a sign-swap, applies q cos plus q-perpendicular sin and the analogous key operation, then uses the standard attention dot product. This is a confirmed implementation link to the rotary relative-position identity.

nodes.label: linear self-attention
nodes.summary: Eqs. 17–19 show how rotation can be inserted without changing the kernelized attention form, supporting the paper's claim that RoPE can be used with linear self-attention.

nodes.label: proof and efficient realization
nodes.summary: Eqs. 20–34 derive intermediate identities and an elementwise implementation of the same pairwise rotation operation that appears in the official pseudocode.

nodes.label: long-term decay
nodes.summary: Eqs. 35–37 decompose the rotary score by frequency and derive a distance-dependent bound; Figure 2 is presented as evidence for the paper's long-term decay discussion.

nodes.label: sequence length flexibility
nodes.summary: The paper presents RoPE as a position mechanism with sequence length flexibility. This is a stated property of the construction and should be kept distinct from any particular downstream benchmark result.

nodes.label: WMT 2014 English-to-German
nodes.summary: Table 1 reports 27.5 BLEU for RoFormer and 27.3 BLEU for Transformer-base. This is an empirical translation result, not part of the algebraic derivation.

nodes.label: Figure 3
nodes.summary: Figure 3 reports faster convergence in the shown language-model pre-training and Performer experiments. The curves are authors-reported and are not digitized in the packet.

nodes.label: GLUE
nodes.summary: Table 2 is mixed in the reported setup: RoFormer is higher on MRPC, STS-B, and QQP, but lower on SST-2, QNLI, and MNLI.

nodes.label: CAIL2019-SCM
nodes.summary: The official fine-tuning script loads one A/B/C legal case, tokenizes A+B and A+C, runs the RoFormer encoder, pools and scores each pair, compares the two sigmoid scores, and retains the best validation epoch. Table 5 reports 68.29 accuracy at length 512 and 69.79 at length 1024.

edges.teaching_point: The first step is not relative position; it is the need to give self-attention any position signal at all.
Shaw et al. move from only absolute position added to token representations toward an attention score that includes a learned relative key term.
Transformer-XL keeps relative position in the attention-score lineage but decomposes content and relative-position interactions.
T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa are presented as retaining, removing, or disentangling terms in the decomposed score, not as the multiplicative construction RoFormer seeks.
The paper's shared diagnosis of prior work is that relative-position methods modify an additive decomposition; this motivates Eq. 11's functional requirement.
The complex-plane construction answers the requirement in two dimensions before the paper generalizes it to feature pairs.
RoPE is the d-dimensional version: every adjacent coordinate pair rotates by a position-dependent phase.
The core algebraic payoff is that absolute rotations are applied to q and k separately, but the attention inner product contains relative position n−m.
The official code does not add a new attention term; it rotates q and k with cos/sin phases and then uses the standard dot product.
The sign-swap and elementwise cos/sin operations in code are the efficient realization of the pairwise rotation identities.
The paper treats RoPE as insertable into linear self-attention by rotating representations while preserving the kernelized attention structure.
Long-term decay is an algebraic analysis of the rotary score by frequency and distance; it should not be conflated with a benchmark metric.
Sequence length flexibility is part of the paper's stated motivation for the rotary formulation, separate from the reported task scores.
The WMT14 result is empirical evidence that the architecture retained comparable translation quality in the reported setup.
Figure 3 concerns optimization trajectories in the shown pre-training and Performer comparisons, not a proof of the rotary identity.
The GLUE table should be read task by task because the reported deltas are mixed rather than uniformly favorable.
In the official long-text fine-tuning script, the RoFormer encoder supplies contextual states; the task decision comes from comparing two pair scores, not from changing the RoPE mechanism.
The 512 versus 1024 result is relevant to long-sequence use, but the packet notes sequential training stages and other confounds.

unresolved.issue: Figure 3 curves are authors-reported and not digitized here, so the packet supports the qualitative statement of faster convergence but not reconstructed numeric curve values.
Table 2 GLUE results are mixed, so the lineage should not teach RoFormer as uniformly better across GLUE tasks.
The CAIL2019-SCM length comparison reports higher accuracy at length 1024 than 512, but sequential training stages and other confounds remain relevant.
Long-term decay is supported by the paper's algebraic bound and Figure 2 discussion; it should not be converted into an empirical benchmark claim beyond those sources.
Only the official README RoPE pseudocode and the CAIL2019-SCM fine-tuning script mappings are confirmed in the packet; no additional repository behavior should be treated as confirmed.

lesson_plan.title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
lesson_plan.promise: Explain the paper's thread from the need for position in self-attention, through the additive relative-position equation lineage, to RoPE's multiplicative rotary position embedding: absolute position is encoded in query and key representations while their inner product depends explicitly on relative position.

sections.title: Why self-attention needs a position signal
sections.intent: Begin with the paper's concern that dot-product self-attention must be given a way to distinguish token order before any relative-position mechanism can matter.
sections.question: How can self-attention know where tokens occur rather than only what their content is?
sections.learning_goal: See position encoding as the prerequisite problem that RoFormer is trying to solve, not as an optional architectural decoration.
sections.misconception: RoPE starts from relative position alone; in the paper's argument, it starts from the broader need to encode position in self-attention.
sections.summary: The Transformer supplies position by adding learned or sinusoidal absolute position before projection. RoFormer asks whether position can instead be injected into query and key representations so that attention scores expose relative position directly.

medium.rationale: The transition from no position signal to additive absolute position is an equation-state change.

sections.title: From additive absolute position to additive and decomposed relative position
sections.intent: Show the historical equation thread that produces the paper's diagnosis: prior methods change the additive decomposition of the attention score rather than replacing it with a multiplicative construction.
sections.question: What changed from Shaw et al. through Transformer-XL, T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa, and what remained additive?
sections.learning_goal: Attribute each related-work equation to its predecessor paper and distinguish learned relative key terms, decomposed content/position interactions, and retained, removed, or disentangled score terms.
sections.misconception: All relative-position methods are the same because they make attention depend on distance; the paper specifically contrasts additive score modifications with RoPE's multiplicative rotation.
sections.summary: Eqs. 5–10 trace relative position encoding through Shaw et al., Transformer-XL, T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa. The common thread, as RoFormer presents it, is an expanded attention score whose terms are added, removed, retained, or disentangled.

medium.rationale: The teaching point is a controlled transition among related attention-score equations.

sections.title: The formulation of relative position encoding
sections.intent: After the lineage has exposed the limits of additive decompositions, state the paper's functional requirement that motivates the rotary construction.
sections.question: Can absolute position be encoded in query and key representations while their inner product depends explicitly on relative position?
sections.learning_goal: Read Eq. 11 as the bridge between prior-work diagnosis and RoPE's mechanism: position enters q and k separately, but the dot product must depend on m−n.
sections.misconception: Eq. 11 is just another additive bias term; it is a functional requirement on transformed query and key representations.
sections.summary: Eq. 11 asks for functions that encode absolute positions into query and key while making their inner product a function of the relative position between token indices. This is the paper's turning point from additive score engineering to multiplicative representation construction.

medium.rationale: The equation changes the problem statement from adding terms to satisfying an inner-product identity.

sections.title: Rotary position embedding: absolute rotations, relative score
sections.intent: Derive and implement the rotary solution at the point where the reader knows the exact requirement it must satisfy.
sections.question: How do separate rotations of q at position m and k at position n collapse to a relative-position term in the attention score?
sections.learning_goal: Understand the two-dimensional complex-plane solution, its d-dimensional feature-pair generalization, and the official pseudocode that realizes the same pairwise rotation before the standard dot product.
sections.misconception: RoPE adds a new relative-position score after attention is computed; the official pseudocode rotates q and k first, then uses the ordinary attention dot product.
sections.summary: Eqs. 12–13 solve the requirement geometrically in two dimensions. Eqs. 14–16 pair feature dimensions and use orthogonality so that separate absolute rotations R_m and R_n produce an inner product containing R at relative position n−m. The official README constructs cos_pos and sin_pos, builds the perpendicular companion by a sign-swap, rotates q and k, and then computes attention with einsum.

medium.rationale: The mechanism is a rotation of adjacent coordinate pairs, and the relative identity is geometric.

sections.title: Linear self-attention, sequence length flexibility, and long-term decay
sections.intent: Separate the algebraic properties of the rotary construction from both the implementation details and the later benchmark claims.
sections.question: What properties does the paper analyze once the rotary identity is established?
sections.learning_goal: Keep linear self-attention compatibility, sequence length flexibility, and long-term decay as paper-stated or algebraically analyzed properties, not as interchangeable empirical metrics.
sections.misconception: Long-term decay or sequence length flexibility is proven by a downstream accuracy table; in the paper thread, long-term decay is derived from the rotary score decomposition and sequence length flexibility is a stated motivation of the construction.
sections.summary: Eqs. 17–19 show that RoPE can be inserted into linear self-attention by rotating representations while preserving the kernelized form. Eqs. 35–37 decompose the rotary score by frequency and derive a distance-dependent bound, with Figure 2 used for the paper's long-term decay discussion.

medium.rationale: Long-term decay uses a controlled result transition from relative distance to a bound; linear self-attention is explained textually because no state change is needed.

sections.title: What the experiments report
sections.intent: Only after the mechanism and its algebraic properties are clear, read the empirical evidence in the context of each task and metric.
sections.question: What does the paper actually report for translation, pre-training convergence, GLUE, and long legal text?
sections.learning_goal: Distinguish exact table values, authors-reported curves, mixed task deltas, and long-sequence settings without treating them as one universal metric.
sections.misconception: RoFormer is uniformly better on every benchmark; the reported GLUE results are mixed, while Table 1 and Figure 3 support narrower claims.
sections.summary: Table 1 reports 27.5 BLEU for RoFormer and 27.3 for Transformer-base on WMT14 English-to-German. Figure 3 reports faster convergence in the shown language-model pre-training and Performer experiments. Table 2 is mixed across GLUE tasks. Table 5 reports 68.29 accuracy at length 512 and 69.79 at length 1024 on CAIL2019-SCM, with relevant training-stage confounds noted.

medium.rationale: Metric transitions are meaningful for Table 1 and the summarized findings, while curve values are not reconstructed beyond the authors-reported qualitative comparison.

sections.title: What not to overclaim
sections.intent: Close by preserving the boundary between exact algebra, confirmed code provenance, authors-reported evidence, and extrapolation claims.
sections.question: Which conclusions are supported directly, and which need caution?
sections.learning_goal: Avoid conflating the Eq. 16 identity with benchmark superiority, Figure 3 curves with digitized numeric data, or the long-sequence table with an isolated causal test.
sections.misconception: Once the rotary identity is proven, all empirical advantages and extrapolation behavior follow automatically.
sections.summary: The algebra shows how RoPE makes the query-key score contain relative position. The official README confirms the q/k rotation implementation. The experiments provide task-specific evidence, but Figure 3 is not digitized here, GLUE deltas are mixed, CAIL2019-SCM length comparisons have confounds, and long-term decay is an algebraic analysis rather than a benchmark metric.

medium.rationale: The cognitive job is provenance and claim-boundary separation, not a state change or geometry.

blocks.label: Why position?
blocks.question: How can self-attention know where tokens occur rather than only what their content is?
blocks.context: RoFormer begins from the Transformer setting: dot-product self-attention must be supplied with a position signal before any formulation of relative position encoding or rotary position embedding can affect attention scores.

blocks.heading: Position encoding is the prerequisite problem
paragraphs: The paper frames position encoding as necessary because self-attention, by itself, compares representations through dot products and does not inherently distinguish the order of tokens in a sequence.

Before introducing rotary position embedding, the paper reviews the Transformer solution: learned or sinusoidal absolute position is added to token representations before the query and key projections. This makes position part of the representations that later participate in attention.

blocks.title: Show the equation-state change from self-attention without an explicit position signal to the Transformer’s additive absolute-position construction before projection.
blocks.intro: Show the equation-state change from self-attention without an explicit position signal to the Transformer’s additive absolute-position construction before projection.

blocks.consequence: Absolute position terms p_m and p_n are added to token representations before W_q and W_k, matching the Eqs. 3–4 construction reviewed by the paper.
beats: Queries and keys are treated as content-derived representations without an explicit position term in the displayed construction.
Absolute position terms p_m and p_n are added to token representations before W_q and W_k, matching the Eqs. 3–4 construction reviewed by the paper.

blocks.heading: What RoFormer changes about the question
paragraphs: The motivating contrast is not “position versus no position” forever; it is how position should enter self-attention. The reviewed Transformer construction makes absolute position additive before projection, while RoFormer asks whether position can be injected into query and key representations so that their inner product exposes relative position directly.

That is why RoPE does not start from relative position alone. In the paper’s argument, relative-position behavior matters only after self-attention has been given a principled way to encode where tokens occur.

blocks.heading: The common thread is additive decomposition, not just “distance awareness”
paragraphs: The misconception to avoid is that all relative-position methods are equivalent because they make attention depend on distance. RoFormer’s related-work section draws a narrower distinction: prior methods change the additive attention-score decomposition, while the paper’s rotary position embedding seeks a multiplicative construction applied to queries and keys before the ordinary dot product.

This distinction sets up the rest of the paper without making an empirical claim. The lineage explains why Eq. 11 is posed; the later rotary equations, linear self-attention compatibility, long-term decay analysis, and benchmark findings are separate parts of the argument.

blocks.prompt: If a method adds q_m^T a^K_{m-n} or a relative bias to the attention score, is it the same kind of construction as RoPE?
blocks.answer: No. In the RoFormer paper's diagnosis, those are additive score modifications. RoPE instead rotates query and key representations so the standard query-key inner product contains relative position.

blocks.label: Eq. 11
blocks.question: Can absolute position be encoded in query and key representations while their inner product depends explicitly on relative position?
blocks.context: The RoFormer paper calls this the formulation of relative position encoding: instead of adding another relative-position term to an attention score, it asks for query and key transformations whose dot product is controlled by the relative index difference.

columns.label: Additive score engineering
columns.question: What was the prior-work pattern diagnosed by the paper?
columns.answer: The paper presents learned or sinusoidal absolute position, Shaw-style relative keys, Transformer-XL, T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa as an equation lineage that modifies an additive or decomposed attention-score expression.

columns.label: Eq. 11 requirement
columns.question: What changes in the RoFormer formulation?
columns.answer: Eq. 11 asks for functions that encode absolute positions into the query and key representations separately while forcing their inner product to be expressible through the relative position m−n.

blocks.title: From additive position terms to the Eq. 11 functional requirement

stages.formula: e_m = x_m + p_m; q_m = W_q e_m, k_n = W_k e_n
stages.intent: Establish the additive construction used by learned and sinusoidal absolute position encodings.
stages.change: Position enters by addition before the query and key projections, so the formulation has not yet required the query-key inner product itself to be a function of relative distance.

stages.paper: Shaw et al., Transformer-XL, T5, TUPE, DeBERTa
stages.formula: a_{m,n} is expanded with relative-position keys, relative-position biases, or disentangled/decomposed score terms.
stages.intent: Show how prior methods insert relative terms into the expanded attention score.
stages.change: Relative position is represented in the attention-score lineage, but the paper diagnoses these methods as still modifying additive or decomposed terms.

stages.formula: ⟨f_q(x_m,m), f_k(x_n,n)⟩ = g(x_m,x_n,m−n)
stages.intent: State the functional requirement that the inner product depend on m−n.
stages.change: The problem statement moves from adding position terms to finding transformed query and key representations whose dot product already carries the relative offset.

stages.formula: f_q(x_m,m) = (W_q x_m)e^{imθ}; f_k(x_n,n) = (W_k x_n)e^{inθ}
stages.intent: Solve the functional requirement in the complex plane and make the rotation geometric.
stages.change: Position becomes a phase rotation, giving the first rotary construction that satisfies the Eq. 11 requirement in two dimensions.

stages.formula: (R_m q)^T(R_n k) = q^T R_{n−m} k
stages.intent: Pair feature dimensions and use orthogonality to obtain R at relative position n−m in the attention score.
stages.change: The two-dimensional construction is generalized to rotary position embedding across adjacent feature pairs.

stages.formula: linear self-attention keeps its kernelized attention form while applying the rotary transformations to the representations.
stages.intent: Show how rotation can be inserted without changing the kernelized attention form.
stages.change: The paper connects the rotary construction to linear self-attention compatibility.

stages.formula: the same pairwise rotation is rewritten through elementwise cos/sin operations and sign-swapped companions.
stages.intent: Prove intermediate identities and derive the efficient realization.
stages.change: These equations elaborate the implementation form rather than changing the Eq. 11 requirement.

stages.formula: the rotary score is decomposed by frequency pairs and bounded as a function of relative distance.
stages.intent: Decompose the rotary score by frequency and bound how it changes with relative distance.
stages.change: The paper’s long-term decay discussion is an algebraic analysis of the rotary score, not the formulation itself.

folded.reason: These equations support the derivation and efficient realization but repeat the same pairwise rotation operation; this formulation section keeps the focus on Eq. 11 and leaves the detailed elementwise implementation to the mechanism/code treatment.

blocks.claim_label: Eq. 11 · formulation of relative position encoding

steps.label: Token content and absolute indices

steps.meaning: The formulation starts with token representations at two absolute sequence positions.

steps.label: Position-aware key map

steps.label: Relative-position constraint
steps.meaning: The required score is a function of token content and the relative offset m−n, which is why Eq. 11 is a functional requirement rather than an additive bias term.

blocks.heading: Eq. 11 is the bridge, not the finished rotary solution
paragraphs: Read Eq. 11 as the paper’s turning point: absolute position may enter the query and key representations, but the dot product must expose relative position through m−n. This is the motivation for the later rotary position embedding construction, where rotations applied separately to q and k make the inner product contain the relative rotation.

blocks.prompt: In Eq. 11, is g(x_m,x_n,m−n) an extra additive bias added after the dot product?
blocks.answer: No. Eq. 11 requires the transformed query-key inner product itself to equal a function of token content and relative position; the rotary construction is introduced later to satisfy that requirement.

blocks.label: RoPE
blocks.question: How do separate rotations of q at position m and k at position n collapse to a relative-position term in the attention score?
blocks.context: The paper’s formulation asks for absolute positions to be encoded into query and key representations while their inner product depends on the relative position between token indices, not on an added relative-position score after attention is computed.

blocks.title: From the relative-position requirement to the rotary mechanism

stages.formula: absolute position is added to token representations before projection
stages.intent: Establish the additive construction used by learned and sinusoidal absolute position encodings.

stages.intent: State the formulation of relative position encoding that the rotary construction must satisfy.

blocks.heading: The relative term appears before attention is scored
paragraphs: The mechanism is multiplicative: RoPE rotates query and key feature pairs before the dot product is taken. The paper’s Eq. 16 shows that absolute rotations at positions m and n reduce to a relative-position rotation at n−m inside the same query-key inner product.

This is the point that prevents a common misreading: RoPE does not add a new relative-position score after attention has already been computed; it changes the representations that enter the ordinary attention score.

blocks.prompt: If RoPE wants a relative-position effect, does the official pseudocode add a new relative-position score after computing attention?
blocks.answer: No. It first rotates q and k with cos/sin feature-pair phases, then computes the ordinary query-key attention score; the relative-position term appears algebraically inside that dot product.

blocks.label: Properties
blocks.question: What properties does the paper analyze once the rotary identity is established?
blocks.context: After Eqs. 14–16 establish the rotary relative-position identity, the paper separates algebraic properties from later benchmark reports: Eqs. 17–19 address linear self-attention compatibility, Sec. 1 motivates sequence length flexibility, and Eqs. 35–37 with Figure 2 support the long-term decay discussion.

columns.label: Linear self-attention

columns.answer: The paper states sequence length flexibility as part of the motivation for the rotary construction, separate from any single downstream accuracy table.

columns.label: Long-term decay
columns.question: What is analyzed?
columns.answer: The paper decomposes the rotary score by frequency and derives a distance-dependent bound in Eqs. 35–37, with Figure 2 used for the long-term decay discussion.

blocks.setting: The official repository's finetune_scm.py pipeline for one A/B/C legal case, where A is paired once with candidate B and once with candidate C.

blocks.takeaway: The official pipeline evaluates a pairwise ranking decision on top of RoFormer contextual states; it does not change the RoPE attention mechanism itself.

blocks.label: Limits
blocks.question: Which conclusions are supported directly, and which need caution?
blocks.context: The safe boundary is: Eq. 16 supports the relative-position identity inside the query-key score; the official README pseudocode confirms the q/k rotation and standard dot product implementation; the experiments provide task-specific evidence; and the long-term decay discussion is an algebraic analysis rather than a benchmark metric.

blocks.heading: The rotary identity is exact, but it is not a universal empirical guarantee
paragraphs: The paper’s algebra shows that separate absolute rotations of query and key can make their inner product contain relative position, specifically the n−m term in Eq. 16. That supports the mechanism claim: rotary position embedding encodes absolute position while the attention score exposes relative position.

The official README scope is narrower than the full repository: the packet confirms the RoPE attention pseudocode that builds cos/sin phases, performs the q/k sign-swap rotation, and then computes the ordinary attention dot product. It also confirms the CAIL2019-SCM fine-tuning script path used to trace the long-text evaluation pipeline.

Those facts do not imply that RoFormer is uniformly better on every benchmark, that Figure 3 provides digitized numerical curves, or that the CAIL2019-SCM length comparison isolates sequence length as the only causal factor.

appendix_entries.title: Why self-attention needs a position signal
appendix_entries.body: The paper’s motivation starts from the fact that dot-product self-attention must be given a mechanism for representing token order. Position encoding is therefore presented as a prerequisite for making attention sensitive to where tokens occur, not as an optional decoration added after the main architecture is complete.

appendix_entries.title: Transformer absolute position in Eqs. 3–4
appendix_entries.body: Eqs. 3–4 are the paper’s reviewed Transformer baseline for position encoding: learned or sinusoidal absolute position is added to the token representation before query and key projection. This additive construction is the immediate reference point for RoFormer’s later question about injecting position into query and key representations differently.

appendix_entries.title: Shaw et al. relative key term in Eq. 5
appendix_entries.body: RoFormer cites Shaw et al. as the step that inserts a learned relative key term into the attention score. In the paper's lineage, this makes attention depend on relative position while preserving an additive score modification.

appendix_entries.title: Transformer-XL decomposition in Eqs. 6–7
appendix_entries.body: RoFormer presents Transformer-XL as expanding the attention score into content and relative-position interactions. The important lineage point is decomposition: relative position appears through added terms rather than through a multiplicative rotation of query and key vectors.

appendix_entries.title: T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa variants in Eqs. 8–10
appendix_entries.body: RoFormer groups T5, TUPE, and DeBERTa as methods that retain, remove, or disentangle terms in the expanded attention score. The paper uses them to show variation within additive or decomposed relative-position formulations.

appendix_entries.title: Why the additive-decomposition diagnosis leads to Eq. 11
appendix_entries.body: The related-work diagnosis is that earlier relative-position methods modify the additive decomposition of the attention score. RoFormer therefore asks a different formulation question in Eq. 11: can absolute positions be encoded into query and key representations so their inner product depends explicitly on relative position?

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 11 as a functional requirement
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 11 states the formulation of relative position encoding: find position-aware query and key functions f_q and f_k such that their inner product can be written as g(x_m,x_n,m−n). The important constraint is on the dot product after transformation, not on adding a new bias term to an already computed score.

appendix_entries.title: Central question: absolute inputs, relative score
appendix_entries.body: The formulation asks whether absolute positions can be encoded into query and key representations while the resulting attention compatibility depends explicitly on the relative position between token indices. This question connects the paper’s diagnosis of additive prior-work formulations to the later rotary position embedding construction.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 12–13: two-dimensional complex-plane solution
appendix_entries.body: The paper first solves the relative-position formulation in two dimensions by using a complex-plane rotation. This makes position a phase rotation rather than an added attention-score term.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 14–16: d-dimensional rotary position embedding
appendix_entries.body: The d-dimensional construction pairs adjacent feature coordinates and applies position-dependent rotations to each pair. The resulting rotary position embedding is the feature-pair generalization of the two-dimensional solution.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 16: relative-position identity
appendix_entries.body: Equation 16 shows the central identity: applying absolute rotations to query and key separately yields an inner product containing a rotation indexed by relative position n−m.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 20–34: proof and efficient pairwise realization
appendix_entries.body: The paper’s intermediate equations derive the elementwise implementation of the same adjacent-pair rotation operation. In the main mechanism section, these equations are folded and linked to the official pseudocode rather than animated one by one.

appendix_entries.title: Official README mapping: rotate q and k before the dot product
appendix_entries.body: The official README constructs cos_pos and sin_pos, builds perpendicular companions for q and k by sign-swap, applies the rotary cosine-plus-sine update, and then computes the standard attention dot product.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 17–19: linear self-attention compatibility
appendix_entries.body: The paper uses Eqs. 17–19 to show that RoPE can be inserted into linear self-attention by rotating representations while preserving the kernelized attention form. This is an algebraic compatibility statement, not an empirical result.

appendix_entries.title: Sequence length flexibility as a stated property
appendix_entries.body: The paper presents sequence length flexibility as part of the motivation for rotary position embedding. In this section it is treated as a stated property of the construction and kept separate from later task-specific results.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 35–37: long-term decay analysis
appendix_entries.body: Eqs. 35–37 decompose the rotary attention score by frequency and derive a distance-dependent bound. The source packet identifies this as the paper's algebraic analysis for long-term decay.

appendix_entries.title: Figure 2 context for long-term decay
appendix_entries.body: Figure 2 is used by the paper in the long-term decay discussion. The packet supports using it as contextual evidence for the derived property, not as a digitized benchmark curve.

appendix_entries.title: WMT14 English-to-German Table 1
appendix_entries.body: Exact source locator: Table 1. The paper reports 27.5 BLEU for RoFormer and 27.3 BLEU for Transformer-base on WMT 2014 English-to-German; this is an exact-table result and should be interpreted within the translation setting and BLEU metric.

appendix_entries.title: Figure 3 convergence curves
appendix_entries.body: Exact source locator: Figure 3. The paper reports faster convergence in the shown language-model pre-training and Performer experiments; the packet supports the qualitative authors-reported curve comparison but not reconstructed numeric curve samples.

appendix_entries.title: GLUE Table 2 mixed task results
appendix_entries.body: Exact source locator: Table 2. The packet identifies the reported GLUE pattern as mixed: RoFormer is higher on MRPC, STS-B, and QQP, but lower on SST-2, QNLI, and MNLI in the reported setup. The table should therefore be read task by task rather than as a uniform improvement claim.

appendix_entries.title: CAIL2019-SCM Table 5 length comparison
appendix_entries.body: Exact source locator: Table 5. The paper reports 68.29 accuracy for RoFormer-512 and 69.79 accuracy for RoFormer-1024 on CAIL2019-SCM. The packet also notes that sequential training stages and other confounds remain relevant when interpreting the length comparison.

appendix_entries.title: Official CAIL2019-SCM fine-tuning pipeline
appendix_entries.body: Exact source locator: finetune_scm.py lines 24-141 in the official repository excerpt. The confirmed mapping loads one A/B/C legal case, emits A+B and A+C paired sequences, runs a RoFormer encoder, pools and scores each pair, compares the two sigmoid scores for a correct-or-incorrect decision, and saves the best validation checkpoint before reporting test accuracy.

appendix_entries.title: Claim boundaries: algebra, code, evidence, and extrapolation
appendix_entries.body: The central boundary is that Eq. 16 establishes the rotary relative-position identity inside the query-key score, while Tables and Figures report task-specific evidence. The identity should not be used as a shortcut to claim uniform benchmark superiority, and the paper’s sequence length flexibility motivation should remain separate from any one downstream score.

appendix_entries.title: Confirmed code scope in this packet
appendix_entries.body: The confirmed implementation evidence is limited to the official README RoPE attention pseudocode and the CAIL2019-SCM fine-tuning script mapping. The README pseudocode constructs cos/sin phases, rotates q and k with a sign-swap companion, and computes the standard attention dot product; the CAIL2019-SCM script mapping traces paired legal-case inputs through scoring and evaluation. Other repository behavior is not established by this packet.

appendix_entries.title: Figure 3 is authors-reported, not digitized here
appendix_entries.body: Figure 3 supports the qualitative statement that the paper reports faster convergence in the shown language-model pre-training and Performer experiments. This packet does not provide digitized curve points, so exact numerical trajectories or reconstructed loss values should not be asserted.

appendix_entries.title: CAIL2019-SCM length comparison needs caution
appendix_entries.body: Table 5 reports 68.29 test accuracy for RoFormer-512 and 69.79 for RoFormer-1024 on CAIL2019-SCM. The packet also flags sequential training stages and other confounds, and the confirmed fine-tuning script shows a paired-input scoring pipeline; therefore the comparison should not be described as an isolated causal test of sequence length alone.

appendix_entries.title: Long-term decay is an algebraic analysis, not a benchmark metric
appendix_entries.body: The long-term decay discussion comes from Eqs. 35–37, which decompose the rotary score by frequency and derive a distance-dependent bound, with Figure 2 used in that analysis. It should be presented as the paper’s algebraic analysis of score behavior with relative distance, not as a measured downstream task score.

dag_nodes.label: Load one A/B/C case
dag_nodes.detail: The label determines which candidate is the positive continuation for case A.

dag_nodes.label: Tokenize A+B and A+C
dag_nodes.detail: The generator emits two paired sequences with labels 1 and 0, then pads the batch.

dag_nodes.label: Run the RoFormer encoder
dag_nodes.detail: build_transformer_model loads model='roformer' before the task head.

dag_nodes.label: Construct cos/sin phases
dag_nodes.detail: The official README repeats the position frequencies across adjacent feature pairs.

dag_nodes.label: Rotate query and key pairs
dag_nodes.detail: Negate-and-swap companions implement q·cos + q⊥·sin and the same operation for k.

dag_nodes.label: Compute query-key scores
dag_nodes.detail: The ordinary einsum now contains the relative-position identity from Eq. 16.

dag_nodes.label: Pool and score each pair
dag_nodes.detail: Global average pooling and a sigmoid head produce one score for A+B and one for A+C.

dag_nodes.label: Compare the pair scores
dag_nodes.detail: Evaluation counts the example correct when the positive pair score exceeds the negative pair score.

dag_nodes.label: Retain the best validation epoch
dag_nodes.detail: The callback saves weights only when validation accuracy improves, then reports test accuracy.
